questions.question: What is the primary function of a Market Category in the mind of a customer?

options.text: It's a way for developers to organize their code
It sets a baseline of expectations for features, price, and competition
It determines which server the software will run on
It is a requirement for filing a business patent

questions.explanation: A category acts as a 'room' that provides context. Once a customer slots you into a category (like 'CRM' vs 'To-Do List'), they immediately have expectations about what you should do and how much you should cost.

questions.question: Which category strategy involves carving out a specific niche where you can be the leader?

options.text: Head-to-Head Strategy
Big Fish, Small Pond (Sub-Segment) Strategy
New Category Strategy
The Commodity Strategy

questions.explanation: The Big Fish, Small Pond strategy (Sub-segmentation) is about becoming the 'Best [X] for [Y]', which is often the most effective path for solo founders.

questions.question: Why is the 'New Category Strategy' (Category Creation) considered risky for solo founders?

options.text: It requires too much custom coding
It's illegal in most jurisdictions
It's expensive and slow because you have to educate the market on a problem they don't know they have
Solo founders aren't allowed to create new categories

questions.explanation: Creating a new category is a 'heavy lift.' It requires significant marketing spend and time to teach people why the old categories are obsolete.

questions.question: What happens to your 'Pricing Power' when you successfully sub-segment a category?

options.text: It decreases because the market is smaller
It stays the same regardless of category
It increases because you are providing specialized value to a specific group
It disappears because you become a commodity

questions.explanation: Specialization leads to higher perceived value. A 'CRM for Dentists' can charge more than a 'Generic CRM' because it solves specific dental business problems.

questions.question: If customers are telling you that your product is 'too expensive,' what is a likely cause according to this lesson?

options.text: Your server costs are too high
You are positioned in a category with a low-price 'anchor'
You aren't offering enough features
The customer doesn't have a credit card

questions.explanation: Perceived value is relative to the 'room' (category) you are in. If you are slotted into a 'Low-Value' category, your price will be anchored to cheap alternatives.

questions.question: Perform a 'Category Swap' for your product. Slot it into a Low-Value, Current, and High-Value category. Which one captures the most value while still being 'honest' to what you built?
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the student's category swap exercise. Check if they understand how changing the 'label' changes the expectations. Provide feedback on whether the 'High-Value' category they chose is actually believable given their features.
